metadata.title: Compound Interest and the Rule of 72
metadata.subtitle: Understand the 'Magic' of Exponential Growth and Make Time Work for You

content.title: Compound Interest and the Rule of 72
content.subtitle: Understand the 'Magic' of Exponential Growth and Make Time Work for You

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
Micah is teaching his younger brother about investing. He wants to show him that if he invests his $100 birthday money at 8% annual return, it would eventually grow to $800.

His brother is skeptical—how can $100 become $800 without adding more money?

Micah needs to explain the 'magic' of compound interest.

How does compound interest turn $100 into $800, and how can you calculate how long this transformation takes?

objectives.description: Explain how compound interest grows money exponentially over time compared to simple interest.
Apply the Rule of 72 to quickly estimate how long it takes money to double at any interest rate.
Calculate the true opportunity cost of spending decisions by considering future value.
Evaluate why starting early matters more than starting with more money.

terms.definition: Interest earned only on the original principal amount. Formula: Principal × Rate × Time.
Interest earned on both the principal AND previously earned interest—creating exponential growth like a snowball.
terms.example: $1,000 at 5% compounded for 10 years = $1,629 (earning interest on interest!)

terms.definition: A quick mental math formula to estimate how long it takes money to double: 72 ÷ Interest Rate = Years to Double.
terms.example: At 8% interest: 72 ÷ 8 = 9 years to double your money.

terms.definition: The future value of money you spend today—what you give up by not investing that money instead.
terms.example: $5/day coffee = $1,800/year. Invested at 7% for 40 years = ~$430,000 opportunity cost!

leftPanel.title: Why Compound Interest Is So Powerful
paragraphs: Albert Einstein reportedly called compound interest 'the eighth wonder of the world.'
Simple interest: You earn interest only on your original deposit. Growth is linear (straight line).
Compound interest: You earn interest on your interest. Growth is exponential (curves upward faster and faster).
The difference seems small at first, but becomes MASSIVE over time.

highlightBox.text: 'He who understands it, earns it. He who doesn't, pays it.' — attributed to Einstein

content.headerTitle: The Rule of 72: Your Mental Math Shortcut

leftPanel.title: 72 ÷ Interest Rate = Years to Double
paragraphs: This simple formula lets you quickly estimate doubling time:

highlightBox.text: You can also flip it: 72 ÷ Years = Required Rate. Need to double in 10 years? You need ~7.2% return.

leftPanel.title: How Many Doublings?
paragraphs: To go from $100 to $800, we need to count doublings:

highlightBox.text: Emma invested 1/3 the money but has $120,000 MORE because she started 10 years earlier!

content.headerTitle: Opportunity Cost: The Hidden Price Tag

layoutData.title: What Does That Purchase REALLY Cost?
paragraphs: Every dollar you spend today has a future value you're giving up:
bulletPoints: $5 daily coffee: $1,800/year × 40 years at 7% = ~$430,000 opportunity cost
$200 sneakers: Invested at 7% for 40 years = ~$3,000 opportunity cost
$30,000 new car vs. $15,000 used: $15,000 difference invested = ~$225,000 in 40 years

keyPoint.text: ⚠️ This doesn't mean never spend money! It means understand the TRUE cost so you can make informed decisions.

content.headerTitle: Compound Interest Works AGAINST You Too

leftPanel.title: Debt Compounds Just Like Savings
paragraphs: The same math that grows your savings can GROW YOUR DEBT:
This is why high-interest debt is an emergency—it's compound interest working against you.

highlightBox.text: Pay off high-interest debt BEFORE investing. A guaranteed 20% 'return' (avoiding CC interest) beats uncertain 7% investment returns.

scenario.name: Micah's Brother's $100
paragraphs: Micah's brother received $100 for his birthday. He's 12 years old. If he invests it at 8% and doesn't touch it until retirement at 67, how much will he have?
That's 55 years of compounding. At 8%, money doubles every 9 years.

scenario.name: Amanda's Coffee Decision
paragraphs: Amanda spends $5/day on coffee. That's $150/month or $1,800/year. She's 25 and plans to work until 65—40 years.
If she invested half that coffee money ($2.50/day = $900/year) at 7% instead, what happens?

content.question:
Use the Rule of 72 to answer:

1. At 6% interest, how long to double your money?

2. You want to double your money in 8 years. What rate do you need?

3. Why might someone choose a 3% savings account (24 years to double) over a 9% investment (8 years to double)?

Think about a regular expense you have (streaming, coffee, eating out, etc.).

• How much do you spend monthly/yearly?
• Using the Rule of 72 at 7%, what would that be worth in 40 years?
• Is the expense worth that opportunity cost to you?

Remember: the answer might be YES—but now it's an informed decision.

questions.question: What makes compound interest more powerful than simple interest?
Using the Rule of 72, how long will it take $5,000 to double at 9% interest?
Why does starting to invest at 22 vs. 32 make such a huge difference, even with less total money invested?
$1,000 at 5% simple interest for 30 years = $2,500. Compound interest = $4,322. How much more does compounding earn?

takeaways.title: Compound Interest Is Exponential
takeaways.description: You earn interest on your interest, creating a snowball effect that accelerates over time.

takeaways.title: Rule of 72 = Quick Math
takeaways.description: 72 ÷ Interest Rate = Years to Double. At 8%, money doubles every 9 years. No calculator needed!

takeaways.title: Time Beats Amount
takeaways.description: Starting early with less money often beats starting late with more. Time is your greatest investing advantage.

takeaways.title: Understand Opportunity Cost
takeaways.description: Every dollar spent today has a future value. Make informed decisions about what's worth the true cost.

main.title: Compound Interest Calculator Challenge
main.description: Micah convinced his brother—but can you convince yourself? Tomorrow you'll calculate real scenarios, compare simple vs. compound interest, and discover the true opportunity cost of your own spending habits.

steps: Calculate Rule of 72 doubling times for various interest rates
Compare simple vs. compound interest growth over 30 years
Determine how many doublings are needed to reach financial goals
Calculate the opportunity cost of a personal spending habit
Create a plan to balance enjoying today while investing for tomorrow

content.tagline: Building Financial Futures, One Lesson at a Time
